verdicts.reasoning: The redirect URL is sourced from get_option('niteoCS_countdown_redirect') which is an admin-only setting, and is sanitized with esc_url() before being used in header('Location: ...'). esc_url() strips javascript: protocol and validates the URL. Only admins can set the option value. No open redirect exploitable by external attackers. File: niteo-cmp.php:930-931

The theme_slug POST parameter is used to construct a URL to CMP_UPDATE_URL (a hardcoded constant), but the function is protected by both check_ajax_referer() (line 1243) and current_user_can('manage_options') (line 1245). Only admins can reach this code. Additionally, the URL is constructed by appending a slug parameter to a fixed base URL - the domain cannot be changed. Not SSRF. File: niteo-cmp.php:1236-1275

Same function as ssrf-001. Protected by nonce + manage_options capability check. The theme_slug is filtered through FILTER_SANITIZE_STRING. The remote_version comes from a response from the plugin's own update server (CMP_UPDATE_URL). wp_die() output is to an admin-only AJAX handler. File: niteo-cmp.php:1243-1306

Same admin-only context as xss-001. The transient HTML construction at line 1299 properly uses esc_attr() for theme_slug and esc_attr() for remote_version within HTML attributes. The function requires manage_options capability. File: niteo-cmp.php:1299-1311

Same admin-only function as xss-001/002. The transient echoed at line 1328 was previously stored via set_transient, and the HTML was constructed with proper escaping (esc_attr). Admin-only context. File: niteo-cmp.php:1271-1328

The email is sanitized with sanitize_email() and then md5-hashed before being used in the Mailchimp API URL. The API key and list_id come from admin-stored options. The URL domain is fixed as '{dc}.api.mailchimp.com'. The function is called from niteo_subscribe which has nonce verification. The email cannot influence the domain. File: niteo-cmp.php:1770-1837

The translation text comes from get_option('niteoCS_translation') which is admin-only stored data. The output is JSON-encoded via json_encode() which escapes HTML entities. The response is sent as an AJAX JSON response, not rendered as HTML. File: niteo-cmp.php:1746-1940

glob() is NOT a command execution function - it is a filesystem pattern matching function. The theme_slug is sanitized with sanitize_text_field() and the function is protected by nonce + publish_pages capability check. Results are further processed through basename() via array_map. No RCE vector. File: niteo-cmp.php:2280-2287

Same admin-only function as rce-001. Output is JSON-encoded and sent via AJAX. The theme_slug is sanitized. URLs are constructed via plugins_url() which returns safe values. File: niteo-cmp.php:2280-2298

The API key goes through sanitize_api_key() which strips all characters except [a-zA-Z0-9-]. The resulting datacenter string (e.g. 'us12') is appended to '.api.mailchimp.com'. The domain is effectively constrained to *.api.mailchimp.com. The function requires publish_pages capability. No SSRF possible. File: niteo-cmp.php:2555-2579, sanitize_api_key at line 2157-2161

The cmp_get_post_detail function is accessible via wp_ajax_nopriv_ but has nonce verification via wp_verify_nonce (line 2873). The $id is passed through esc_attr(). The post data returned via wp_send_json() comes from WordPress core functions (get_the_title, get_the_date, etc.) which return sanitized content. The content is JSON-encoded. File: niteo-cmp.php:2869-2898

The echo outputs the result of in_array() ternary: either the literal string 'selected' or empty string ''. The tainted data ($niteoCS_page_whitelist) is used only as an argument to in_array(), never echoed directly. The output is always a safe literal. File: cmp-advanced.php:246-339
Same pattern as xss-007. in_array() returns boolean, ternary outputs 'selected' or ''. No user data is echoed. File: cmp-advanced.php:246-342
Same safe in_array() ternary pattern. Outputs literal 'selected' or ''. File: cmp-advanced.php:248-361
Same safe in_array() ternary pattern. File: cmp-advanced.php:248-364
Same safe in_array() ternary pattern. Outputs 'selected' or ''. File: cmp-advanced.php:251-445

The variable $bypass is from get_option but the echo uses $bypass_id (a different variable). Even if $bypass were used, the admin settings page context means only admins see it. The echo outputs a constructed URL with get_home_url(). File: cmp-advanced.php:255-498
Same safe in_array() ternary pattern outputting 'selected' or ''. Admin page context. File: cmp-advanced.php:251-668
The output uses esc_attr() on the value. Admin settings page context. File: cmp-advanced.php:253-757
Same as xss-014. Output uses esc_attr(). Admin page context. File: cmp-advanced.php:254-789

This is a settings page file (settings-counter.php) that is only included/rendered in the WordPress admin area for users with appropriate capabilities. The scanner incorrectly marked auth_required as false because the file itself doesn't contain auth checks - the auth is enforced by the parent admin page loading mechanism. The email address is from get_option which is admin-set. File: inc/settings/settings-counter.php:104
Admin settings page context. Value is output with esc_url() sanitization. File: inc/settings/settings-counter.php:36-115

Admin settings page context (settings-content.php). The output uses niteo_sanitize_html() which applies wp_kses() for non-admin users. This is intentional behavior - allowing admins to set rich HTML content for the coming soon page body. File: inc/settings/settings-content.php:34-58

The output is a ternary that echoes either '' or 'disabled' based on loose comparison of $niteoCS_banner with '1' or '0'. No user-controlled data is echoed. The tainted variable is only used in a boolean comparison. Admin settings page context. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-45
Same safe ternary pattern as xss-019. Outputs '' or ' disabled'. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-51
Same safe ternary pattern. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-73
Same safe ternary pattern. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-79
Same safe ternary pattern. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-86
Same safe ternary pattern. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-94
Same safe ternary pattern. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-102
Same safe ternary pattern. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-107
Same safe ternary pattern. Outputs 'disabled' or ''. File: inc/settings/settings-slider.php:28-110

Admin settings page (settings-subscribe.php). The image ID comes from get_option, is passed to wp_get_attachment_image_src() which returns a WP-controlled URL, and is output with esc_url(). Properly sanitized. File: inc/settings/settings-subscribe.php:150-259
Same pattern as xss-028. Admin page, esc_url() applied. File: inc/settings/settings-subscribe.php:150-293
The echo outputs count($niteoCS_subscribers_list) or '0'. count() returns an integer. No user data is echoed. File: inc/settings/settings-subscribe.php:134-313
Safe ternary pattern. Outputs 'block' or 'none' based on isset/is_array check. No user data echoed. Admin settings page. File: inc/settings/settings-background.php:55-229

Admin settings page (settings-footer.php). Output uses niteo_sanitize_html() which applies wp_kses() for non-admin users. This is intended for rich content. File: inc/settings/settings-footer.php:54-66
Admin settings page (settings-footer.php). Output uses niteo_sanitize_html(). Same as xss-032. File: inc/settings/settings-footer.php:87-93
Frontend render file (login-icon.php) but the output uses esc_url() which sanitizes the URL. The login URL comes from admin-stored get_option. Properly escaped. File: inc/render/login-icon.php:5-12

This outputs admin-stored analytics code (e.g., Google Tag Manager, Facebook Pixel) via echo stripslashes(). This is by design - the admin intentionally stores arbitrary JS code for analytics tracking. WordPress admins already have the unfiltered_html capability. The stored XSS vector requires admin access to set the option in the first place. File: inc/render/head-scripts.php:59-60
The output is round($counter_date) + 50 which is always a numeric value. round() returns a float, addition produces a number. No string injection possible. File: inc/render/counter.php:9-17
Output uses esc_url(). The image URL comes from wp_get_attachment_image_src() which returns WP-controlled URLs. Properly sanitized. File: inc/render/seo.php:9-27
Same as xss-037. esc_url() applied. File: inc/render/seo.php:9-34

The favicon URL comes from wp_get_attachment_image_src() which returns WordPress-managed URLs. While the output at line 43 does not use esc_url() on $favicon_url[0], the value is sourced from wp_get_attachment_image_src() which only returns URLs from the WordPress uploads directory. An attacker would need admin access to set the option to a non-integer value, but wp_get_attachment_image_src returns false for invalid IDs. Admin-only stored option. File: inc/render/seo.php:38-43

uasort() with a hardcoded callback array($this,'sort_social') is NOT RCE. The callback is fixed in the source code and cannot be influenced by user input. The scanner incorrectly flagged uasort() as a dangerous sink. The social media data from get_option is used as the array to sort, not as the callback. File: inc/render/social-icons.php:9-12
Safe ternary pattern. Outputs ' no-img' or '' based on comparison. No user data echoed. File: inc/render/footer.php:9-24
Output uses esc_url() sanitization. URL from wp_get_attachment_image_src(). File: inc/render/footer.php:9-26

The $firstname variable is coerced to boolean (true/false) via ternary. It's passed as a boolean parameter to cmp_get_form(). The tainted option value is never used as a string in output. File: inc/render/footer.php:12-35

The optin_id is passed through absint() before being used, which ensures it's a non-negative integer. The HTML comes from the MailOptin plugin's form builder which handles its own escaping. File: inc/render/footer.php:84-92
Safe ternary pattern. The $top variable is compared to '0', outputting '' or esc_attr($radius). The output is always either empty or esc_attr-sanitized. File: inc/render/custom-css.php:135-154
Same safe ternary pattern as xss-044. File: inc/render/custom-css.php:135-155
Output uses esc_url() on the YouTube URL. Properly sanitized. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:96
Output uses esc_url(). Source is wp_get_attachment_image_src(). File: inc/render/javascripts.php:74-100
Safe ternary pattern. Outputs literal 'true' or 'false'. No user data echoed. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:69-103
Output uses esc_url(). Same source as xss-047. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:74-116
Same safe ternary as xss-048. Outputs 'true' or 'false'. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:69-117
Output uses esc_url(). URL from wp_get_attachment_url(). File: inc/render/javascripts.php:123-129
Output uses esc_url(). Same source as xss-047/049. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:74-132
Same safe ternary as xss-048/050. Outputs 'true' or 'false'. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:69-134
Output uses esc_url(). Admin-stored option. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:236-241

The hex2rgba() function converts a hex color string to RGBA format. It parses hex digits only. Even if the option value contains malicious content, hex2rgba() would return malformed but non-executable CSS color values. Admin-stored option. File: inc/render/javascripts.php:373
Output uses esc_url(). The Unsplash download URL comes from a remote API response, not directly from user input. File: inc/render/graphic-background.php:73-110

The $_GET['background'] value at graphic-slider.php:5 is passed to cmp_background() as $niteoCS_banner parameter. Inside graphic-background.php (line 9), the value is processed: if it equals '1' it becomes 0, otherwise it's passed through esc_attr(). The value is then used in a switch statement (line 27) with cases '0'-'7'. Non-matching values fall to default:break with no output. The switch uses loose comparison (==) which means only numeric-like strings match. No XSS vector - user input never reaches HTML output unescaped. File: inc/render/graphic-slider.php:4-5, inc/render/graphic-background.php:8-9,27

The $imgs variable contains URLs from the Unsplash API response (json_decoded from $unsplash['body']). These are server-to-server API response URLs, not user-controlled input. The slider_count from get_option only affects how many images are fetched, not the URL content. The json_encode at line 92 properly escapes the data for JavaScript context. File: inc/render/graphic-slider.php:10-108

summary: All 64 automated findings are false positives. The scanner suffered from three systematic issues: (1) misidentifying admin settings pages as unauthenticated because auth checks are in the parent page loader rather than the included template files, (2) flagging safe ternary expressions that output literal strings ('selected', 'disabled', 'true', 'false', 'block', 'none') where user data is used only in boolean comparisons, and (3) flagging properly sanitized outputs using esc_url(), esc_attr(), wp_kses(), and json_encode(). The plugin has three wp_ajax_nopriv_ handlers (cmp_get_post_detail, niteo_subscribe, cmp_disable_comingsoon_ajax) which were all verified to have adequate protections including nonce verification and input sanitization.
feedback: Scanner has a near-100% FP rate on this plugin. Key improvements needed: (1) Recognize that in_array() ternary patterns outputting literals are safe sinks. (2) Track auth context through include chains - settings page templates inherit auth from their admin page parents. (3) Recognize esc_url(), esc_attr(), json_encode(), absint(), and round() as effective sanitizers. (4) Do not flag uasort() with hardcoded callbacks as RCE. (5) Do not flag count() output as XSS.
